title: Vanilla buttercream
subtitle: American-style buttercream for layering and finishing cakes
excerpt: The workhorse frosting: icing sugar beaten into softened butter with a splash of milk and vanilla. It pipes, it spreads, it holds its shape at room temperature. The method is ten minutes; the results scale from a thin crumb coat to a thick decorative border.
sourceNotes: American-style buttercream (icing sugar beaten into fat) appears in domestic baking manuals from the late nineteenth century as a standard cake finish. The creaming method using refined icing sugar and butter is documented in Fannie Merritt Farmer, Boston Cooking-School Cook Book (1896), a public-domain text freely available via Project Gutenberg. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, records similar sugar-and-fat fillings under 'ornamental cake icing'. The American powdered-sugar style was standardised across the twentieth century and is now the most widely reproduced domestic buttercream in the English-speaking world.

recipe.yieldDescription: enough to fill and frost a 2-layer 20 cm cake
recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in an airtight container for up to 3 months. Defrost overnight in the fridge; beat again briefly before using.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles or halves cleanly. Scale with the cake.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps at room temperature for 2 days, in the fridge for 1 week. Beat briefly before using if it has chilled.

baking.decoratingTechnique: buttercream — crumb coat, then finish coat

glossaryTerms.definition: A thin first layer of buttercream applied to a cake and chilled before the final coat. Its purpose is to trap crumbs so they do not contaminate the visible outer layer. Apply thinly with an offset spatula, refrigerate for 15 to 30 minutes until firm, then apply the final coat. Skipping the crumb coat produces a frosted exterior speckled with crumbs.

content.text: American buttercream is the fastest frosting to make and the most forgiving to pipe. The ratio is 2 parts icing sugar to 1 part butter by weight. Adjust the consistency with milk: stiffer for piped borders, slightly looser for a smooth spread finish.

items.prepNote: plus a little more if needed

content.text: Beat the softened {{unsalted-butter}} in a stand mixer or with a hand mixer for 3 to 4 minutes on medium-high speed until pale and fluffy. Cold butter will not aerate; if you are short of time, cut the butter into small cubes and leave it in a warm kitchen for 20 minutes.

With the mixer on low, add the sifted {{icing-sugar}} in three additions, beating well between each. Adding it all at once produces a dust cloud and an uneven mix. Scrape the bowl down once before the final addition.

Add the {{vanilla-extract}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{whole-milk}}. Beat on medium for 2 minutes. Check the consistency: for piping rosettes and borders, it should hold a stiff peak; for spreading, it should be smooth and just drop off the spatula. Add milk a teaspoon at a time to loosen, or a little extra icing sugar to stiffen.
Place the cake on a turntable or a plate. Apply a

with the icing spatula, spreading thinly and evenly over the top and sides. Refrigerate for 20 minutes. Apply the final coat generously, smoothing with the icing spatula held at a shallow angle. For a very smooth finish, dip the spatula in hot water and wipe dry before each pass.
